sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 7 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 25, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Agent is at rows 18-23, aligned with Red block (rows 19-22). Green block was collected and is now at rows 31-34, cols 18-21 (doesn't move with agent). Red and Blue blocks still on right wall. Need to collect Red next.
- Goal model: Navigate the agent to connect to colored blocks in the order shown in bottom panel: Red → Green → Blue.
- Action model: UP/DOWN moves agent vertically by 6 rows. RIGHT extends the path rightward. Need to test what happens when path reaches a colored block.
- Plan: Extend RIGHT to reach Red block and collect it.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 24 of about 61 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the path has extended and reached the Red block! The Red block is now connected to the path. But the Red block is still at cols 42-45 according to the segmentation. Let me check if it was collected or not.
Looking at the image more carefully:
- The path extends from the agent to the Red block
- The Red block appears to be at the end of the path
- The Blue block is still below the Red block
- The Green block is below the agent
But the segmentation shows Red is still at cols 42-45. So maybe the Red block hasn't been collected yet. Looking at the image, the path reaches the Red block but maybe I need one more RIGHT to actually "touch" it and collect it.
Wait, actually looking at the image, the path seems to connect to the Red block. The Red block is at the end of the path. Maybe I need to press RIGHT one more time to fully collect it.
Let me check the bottom panel - does it show any changes? The bottom panel still shows Pink, Red, Green, Blue. Maybe the Red block needs to be "touched" by the path extending through it.
Let me try pressing RIGHT a few more times to see if the Red block gets collected.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Path extended to reach Red block. The Red block is at the end of the path but hasn't been collected yet (still at cols 42-45). Need to extend further to collect it.
Plan: Press RIGHT more to fully collect the Red block, then move to collect Blue.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Extend RIGHT more to collect Red
predict(expect_change=True, note='Extend RIGHT to collect Red block')
action(['RIGHT'] * 5)

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("=== After more RIGHTs ===")
print("Colored blocks:")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['R', 'b', 'N'] and n['pixels'] == 16:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        r = min(rows); c = min(cols)
        if r < 50:
            print(f"  color={n['color']} rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Check path end
print("\nPath tiles near Red:")
for n in seg:
    if n['pixels'] <= 4 and n['color'] in ['w', 'g']:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        r = min(rows); c = min(cols)
        if 16 <= r <= 26 and c >= 38:
            print(f"  color={n['color']} pos=({r},{c})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  color=b rows 25-28, cols 42-45
  color=N rows 31-34, cols 18-21

  color=g pos=(20,38)
  color=w pos=(20,38)
  color=g pos=(20,41)
  color=w pos=(20,46)
  color=g pos=(21,40)
  color=w pos=(21,41)
  color=g pos=(21,46)
